content.assessment: Reforming admissions mechanisms while preserving the existing track network leaves geographic concentration and feeder-network contraction untouched, but this proposal's network burden is lower since it does not require new capacity or transport reconfiguration — it operates within the existing physical network.
opportunities: Prior administrative desegregation measures showed admissions-level interventions can shift network-relevant outcomes without new infrastructure
strengths: Does not require altering network geography, capacity, or transport infrastructure, since the 880-school network and its access design remain unchanged
Can be implemented within existing institutional sites without new capacity investment
threats: Historical precedent of desegregation measures 'stagnating mid-decade' suggests admissions reform effects may not persist within the existing network structure
Centralized KLIK control could both enable uniform admissions rule changes and simultaneously block local network adaptation to rural capacity constraints
weaknesses: Church-maintained and private schools sit outside KLIK authority, creating a coverage gap in any nationally uniform admissions reform, limiting network-wide consistency
Does not address geographic concentration of tracks in Budapest and regional centers or the contracting feeder network
